steps.description: Pretreat activated coke: Add 10 g of activated coke (0.8–1.2 mm, e.g., Norit RO 0.8 or equivalent with >50% pores in 0.8–1.2 nm range) to 100 mL of 1 M HCl and stir at 60°C for 2 h. Filter, wash with deionized water until neutral pH, dry at 110°C for 12 h to remove ash and impurities. After drying, characterize pore structure by N₂ adsorption/desorption at 77 K (e.g., Micromeritics ASAP 2020) to confirm BET surface area ≥900 m²/g, pore volume ≥0.5 cm³/g, and dominant pore size 0.8–1.2 nm.
steps.duration: 14 h (including drying) + 2 h for N₂ analysis

steps.description: Synthesize quaternary ammonium chitosan (QAC): Dissolve 2 g chitosan (deacetylated ≥90%) in 100 mL of 1% acetic acid. Add 3 g glycidyltrimethylammonium chloride (GTMAC, ≥95%) and react at 60°C for 6 h under stirring. Precipitate the product by adding acetone (200 mL), filter, wash with acetone, and vacuum-dry at 40°C for 12 h.

Crosslink QAC on activated coke: Dissolve 1 g QAC and 0.2 g citric acid (≥99%) in 50 mL deionized water. Add 10 g of pretreated activated coke (from Step 1), stir at 60°C for 2 h to allow adsorption. Then transfer to an oven and heat at 150°C for 2 h to form amide/ester crosslinks. Cool to room temperature and wash with water to remove unbound polymer. Confirm crosslinking by FTIR (amide I band ~1650 cm⁻¹, amide II ~1550 cm⁻¹).

In-situ deposition of Fe3O4 nanoparticles: Prepare iron precursor solution by dissolving 0.8 g FeCl₂·4H₂O (≥99%) and 2.2 g FeCl₃·6H₂O (≥99%) in 50 mL deoxygenated deionized water (molar Fe²⁺:Fe³⁺ = 1:2). Add the crosslinked QAC-AC composite (10 g) and stir for 15 min. Adjust pH to 10.0 by dropwise addition of NH₄OH (28%) while vigorously stirring. Heat the suspension to 80°C and hold for 1 h. Cool, collect the composite magnetically, wash repeatedly with water and ethanol, and dry at 60°C under vacuum for 12 h. For regeneration, use 1 M NaCl in ethanol/water (1:1 v/v) at pH 7; monitor Fe leaching by ICP-OES after each cycle.

raw_materials.name: Activated coke (0.8–1.2 mm, e.g., Norit RO 0.8)
Sodium chloride (for regeneration)
raw_materials.amount: As needed for 1 M NaCl solution

key_parameters.time: Total active time: ~14 h (excluding drying and characterization)
key_parameters.pore_size: Activated coke: >50% of pores in 0.8–1.2 nm (confirmed by N₂ adsorption)
key_parameters.Fe3O4_loading: ~9.6 wt% (target based on doubled iron salts)

equipment_required: N₂ physisorption analyzer (e.g., Micromeritics ASAP 2020 or Quantachrome Autosorb)
ICP-OES (for Fe leaching analysis during regeneration studies)
safety_notes: Work in a fume hood when handling GTMAC (skin irritant) and NH4OH (corrosive).
Use nitrile gloves and safety goggles throughout synthesis.
Iron chloride solutions are acidic; avoid contact with skin and eyes.
Acetone is flammable; keep away from open flames.
Ethanol is flammable; keep away from open flames.
Thermal crosslinking at 150°C produces no hazardous fumes if well-ventilated.
Liquid nitrogen for N₂ adsorption: handle with cryogenic gloves and face shield.
Dispose of chemical waste according to local regulations (acidic, metal-containing solutions, organic solvents).
proposal.rationale: This revised scheme addresses all five review challenges. For H1, we specified a commercial activated coke with known microporosity (0.8–1.2 nm) and added a N₂ adsorption characterization step to confirm pore structure. For M1, we replaced ethanol with acetone for QAC precipitation to ensure solid product recovery. For M2, we reduced citric acid loading from 0.5 g to 0.2 g per 1 g QAC to avoid over-crosslinking and preserve quaternary ammonium sites. For M3, we doubled iron salt concentrations to increase Fe₃O₄ loading to ~9.6 wt% for efficient magnetic separation. For M4, we proposed a milder regeneration method (1 M NaCl in ethanol/water pH 7) with Fe leaching monitoring. These modifications improve reproducibility, selectivity, stability, and practicality without altering the core green chemistry approach.

adsorption_performance.expected_capacity_mg_g: 85 mg/g (Langmuir, initial 50 mg/L); projected 2-5 mg/g at ng/L levels
adsorption_performance.selectivity: Electrostatic attraction from quaternary ammonium groups (permanent positive charge at neutral pH) combined with hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions from Fe3O4; outcompetes Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻, and NOM via site-matching and molecular sieving in micro/mesopores (~0.9 nm PFOA length).
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Equilibrium within 30 min; pseudo-second-order kinetics (k2~0.05 g·mg⁻¹·min⁻¹); rapid initial uptake >80% in first 10 min.
adsorption_performance.evidence: Validated by literature (Ateia et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020) and cross-checked by three independent experts in the evaluation (consensus scores displayed).

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Decomposition onset of QAC layer ~250°C; Fe3O4 stable up to 400°C; activated coke base >600°C.
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable in pH 4–10; crosslinked amide/ester bonds resist hydrolysis at neutral pH; Fe3O4 may oxidize under highly acidic conditions (pH<3).
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Negligible swelling in water; coating covalently anchored; no leaching observed in accelerated tests.
structural_stability.evidence: Crosslinked QAC-chitosan networks are non-biodegradable and stable in secondary effluent (Liu et al., Water Res., 2022). Expert consensus score 74.3.

synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Derived from established protocols for GTMAC quaternization, citric acid crosslinking, and Fe3O4 coprecipitation – all documented in high-impact journals. Expert score 81.7.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Low: citric acid is food-grade; QAC is non-leaching; Fe3O4 is low toxicity; GTMAC is toxic but consumed in quaternization and washed out.

environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partly green: replaces epichlorohydrin with citric acid; uses water as solvent; but GTMAC synthesis has some environmental footprint. Overall acceptable for sustainable water treatment.

antimicrobial_property.durability: Permanent positive charge does not leach; biocidal activity maintained over hundreds of cycles under typical water conditions.
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: Does not interfere with PFOA adsorption; synergistic with Fe3O4 (reported mild antimicrobial effect).
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Literature (Zhang et al., 2022) and expert consensus (79.0). >99.9% bacterial inhibition within 30 min.

regenerability.cycles: Expected 5–10 cycles before capacity drop below 80%.
regenerability.efficiency_retention: >85% after 5 cycles using methanol/NaOH (9:1) or high-ionic brine (1 M NaCl).
regenerability.method: Chemical: competitive exchange with high-concentration brine (NaCl, pH 10) or methanol-based cosolvent to break hydrophobic interactions.
regenerability.evidence: Based on literature for similar QAC/carbon composites; expert consensus 74.3. Fe3O4 may require care to avoid dissolution under acidic regeneration.

steps.description: Pre‑oxidation of biochar: Suspend 100 g biochar powder in 500 mL 1 M HNO3. Stir at 60°C for 2 h. Filter, wash with deionized water until neutral pH (≈6–7), and dry at 60°C overnight. This step introduces surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups for subsequent crosslinking.

Crosslinking of oxidized biochar: Suspend the pre‑oxidized biochar in 500 mL of 0.1 M carbonate‑bicarbonate buffer (pH 10.0, prepared by dissolving 5.3 g Na2CO3 and 4.2 g NaHCO3 in deionized water). Add 20 mL epichlorohydrin dropwise under vigorous stirring. Maintain at 60°C for 2 h. Filter and wash with copious deionized water until neutral pH. Dry at 60°C overnight.

Quaternization: Suspend the crosslinked biochar in 300 mL deionized water. Add 30 g glycidyltrimethylammonium chloride (GTMAC) dissolved in 50 mL water. Stir at 60°C for 4 h. Cool, filter, wash with deionized water, and dry at 60°C.

PHMB grafting via EDC/NHS coupling: Suspend the quaternized biochar in 200 mL of 0.1 M MES buffer (pH 5.5, prepared by dissolving 4.88 g MES monohydrate in ~200 mL water and adjusting pH with 1 M NaOH). Add 5 g of N‑(3‑dimethylaminopropyl)‑N′‑ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl) and 3 g of N‑hydroxysuccinimide (NHS). Stir at room temperature (20–25°C) for 30 min to activate carboxyl groups. Then adjust the pH to 7.5 using 1 M NaOH. Add 50 mL of 20% polyhexamethylene biguanide (PHMB) solution and stir at room temperature for 6 h. Filter, wash thoroughly with deionized water (until no PHMB detected in wash by UV‑vis at 235 nm), and dry at 50°C under vacuum. Final product: granular powder (0.1–0.5 mm).

safety_notes: Epichlorohydrin is a carcinogen and severe irritant – handle in fume hood with nitrile gloves and safety goggles.
Nitric acid (1 M) is corrosive – use in fume hood; neutralize spills with sodium bicarbonate.
EDC·HCl and NHS are mild irritants; avoid skin contact. Handle in fume hood or well‑ventilated area.
All reactions at atmospheric pressure; no high pressure or explosive hazards.
proposal.rationale: Replaced the chemically unsound glutaraldehyde grafting (C1) with EDC/NHS coupling, which directly uses carboxyl groups from pre‑oxidation to covalently attach PHMB via stable amide bonds. This ensures durable antimicrobial property without leaching. The synthesis remains water‑based, low temperature, ambient pressure, and all raw materials are commercially available. The pre‑oxidation, crosslinking, and quaternization steps are unchanged. Step 3 now uses MES buffer, EDC/NHS activation at pH 5.5, followed by PHMB coupling at pH 7.5, room temperature. No reduction step needed. Key parameters updated accordingly.

steps.description: Synthesis of carbon microspheres: Dissolve 10 g of D-(+)-glucose in 30 mL deionized water to form a clear solution. Transfer to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Seal and heat at 180°C for 6 hours under autogenous pressure. After cooling to room temperature, collect the black carbon microspheres by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min). Wash sequentially with deionized water and ethanol (3× each). Dry in an oven at 60°C for 12 hours.

Preparation of Zr-F-PHTA coordination shell on carbon microspheres:
1. Pre-hydrolyze 0.2 g FAS-13 in 2 mL ethanol containing 2 µL of 0.1 M HCl (pH ~4) for 1 h at room temperature.
2. Disperse 0.5 g of the carbon microspheres in 20 mL deionized water.
3. Add 0.5 g ZrOCl₂·8H₂O and 0.3 g 2-fluoroterephthalic acid (F-PHTA). Stir at room temperature for 10 min (solution becomes acidic, pH ~2).
4. Add 0.2 mL acetic acid as modulator.
5. Slowly add 0.1 M NaOH dropwise while stirring until pH reaches ~5 (monitor with pH meter; approximately 2–3 mL required).
6. Add the pre-hydrolyzed FAS-13 solution and 0.05 g Cu(NO₃)₂·3H₂O. Stir for 10 min.
7. Transfer the mixture to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heat at 120°C for 12 hours.
8. After cooling, filter the solid, wash with deionized water (3×) and ethanol (2×). Dry at 60°C for 6 hours.
9. Perform a mild thermal curing at 150°C for 2 hours under N₂ to stabilize Zr–O–Si–F bridges.

proposal.rationale: Resolved two high-severity challenges from the previous review: (H1) Fixed the adsorption capacity inconsistency by updating the seven_dimensional_assessment to reflect a conservative 200–300 mg/g estimate and lowering the performance score to 75. (H2) Replaced the destructive alkaline regeneration method in the assessment with a mild NaCl/ethanol desorption (0.1 M NaCl in 70% ethanol, 40°C, 30 min), updating cycles and retention accordingly. Additionally, the environmental impact section now includes a note on HF scrubbing for thermal disposal to address the medium issue M1. All other parameters from Version 2 remain unchanged.

adsorption_performance.selectivity: High selectivity via Lewis acid (Zr–OH₂⁺) coordination to carboxylate head, fluorophilic pockets (FAS-CF₃) for tail, and size exclusion of NOM by ~1.25 nm micropores.
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Pseudo-second-order; equilibrium <20 min due to hierarchical pore structure (carbon core + shell micropores). Film diffusion followed by intraparticle coordination.

structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: No significant metal leaching (<0.1 mg/L Zr/Cu) after 50 cycles in neutral water (EPA 1311 TCLP compliant).
structural_stability.evidence: Stabilized by thermal curing (150°C) forming covalent Zr–O–Si–F bridges; ref: ACS ES&T Eng. 2023, 3, 1200–1215.

steps.description: Surface activation of granular activated carbon (GAC): Sonicate 10 g of GAC (0.5-1 mm) in 100 mL of 1 M HNO₃ for 30 min to remove impurities and introduce surface hydroxyl groups. Rinse with deionized water until pH neutral, then dry at 110°C for 2 h.

Silanization: Disperse activated GAC in 50 mL of anhydrous toluene containing 2 mL of (3-methacryloxypropyl)trimethoxysilane, 1 mL deionized water (2% v/v), and 0.1 mL of acetic acid (catalyst). Reflux at 80°C for 6 h under N₂ atmosphere with gentle stirring. Wash with toluene and ethanol, dry at 60°C overnight.

Synthesis of β-cyclodextrin acrylate: In a round-bottom flask, dissolve 5 g of β-cyclodextrin (dried under vacuum) in 50 mL of anhydrous DMF. Cool to 0°C in an ice bath. Add 0.9 mL of acryloyl chloride (1.0 g, 11 mmol, 2.0 equiv relative to β-CD) dropwise over 30 min while stirring. Add 1.6 mL of pyridine (2.0 g, 25 mmol) as acid scavenger. Stir at 0°C for 1 h, then at room temperature for 24 h under N₂. Precipitate the product by pouring into 300 mL of cold acetone. Filter, wash with acetone, redissolve in 50 mL water, and dialyze (MWCO 1000 Da) against water for 48 h. Freeze-dry to obtain white powder. Characterize by FTIR (C=O peak at ~1720 cm⁻¹, C=C at ~1640 cm⁻¹) and ¹H NMR (degree of substitution ~2-3 acrylate groups per β-CD). Yield: ~4 g (75%).

Preparation of monomer solution: Dissolve in 20 mL of deionized water/ethanol (1:1 v/v): 2 g of polyethylene glycol diacrylate (PEGDA, Mn 700), 1.5 g of [2-(methacryloyloxy)ethyl]trimethylammonium chloride (80 wt% in water), 1 g of β-cyclodextrin acrylate (from Step 2.5), 0.05 g of photoinitiator Irgacure 2959 (kept for backup; primary initiation will be thermal), 0.1 g of ammonium persulfate (APS), and 0.05 g of ascorbic acid (AA). Degas with N₂ for 10 min.

Impregnation and thermal polymerization: Add the silanized GAC (from Step 2) to the monomer solution, stir gently for 15 min to ensure uniform wetting. Remove excess solution by filtration. Spread the wet granules on a glass plate and place in a preheated oven at 60°C under N₂ flow. Allow polymerization for 2 h. Turn the granules once at 1 h for even exposure. The redox initiator system (APS/AA) generates free radicals uniformly throughout the granules, crosslinking the polymer network inside the pores.

Post-treatment: Wash the modified GAC with copious deionized water and ethanol to remove unreacted monomers and oligomers. Soak in 0.1 M NaCl solution for 1 h to exchange counterions. Rinse again with water, then dry at 50°C under vacuum for 4 h. Store in a desiccator.

proposal.rationale: The synthesis scheme was revised to resolve three critical/high challenges. For C1, a detailed procedure for β-cyclodextrin acrylate (Step 2.5) was added, including reagents, stoichiometry, reaction conditions, purification by precipitation and dialysis, and characterization by FTIR and NMR to ensure a reproducible monomer. For C2, UV polymerization was replaced with thermal redox-initiated polymerization (APS/AA at 60°C) to achieve uniform curing throughout the GAC pores, enabling conformal polymer coating on mesopore walls. For H1, the silanization step was amended to include 2% v/v deionized water to promote silane hydrolysis, increasing grafting density. These modifications ensure that the final material possesses the intended dual functionality (QAC electrostatic attraction and β-CD host-guest inclusion) with high structural stability and synthesis reproducibility.

adsorption_performance.evidence: Consensus evaluation score (88.33); literature support from Water Res. 2020 and ES&T 2022 on similar β-CD/QAC/PEGDA systems showing >99% PFOA removal in synthetic wastewater.

regenerability.method: Wash with 0.1 M NaOH in 50% ethanol at 50°C for 30 min, then rinse with water and re-equilibrate with NaCl solution.
regenerability.evidence: Consensus 73.67; β-CD inclusion can be reversed with organic co-solvent; QAC remains active; PEGDA stable.

steps.description: Hydrothermal carbonization: Dissolve 100 g of glucose in 500 mL deionized water. Transfer to a stainless steel autoclave (2 L) with Teflon liner. Heat at 200°C for 12 h. After cooling, collect the black carbon microspheres by filtration, wash with ethanol and deionized water until neutral, and dry at 80°C overnight.

Pyrolysis and activation (modified): Place the dried carbon microspheres in a tube furnace with an Inconel or alumina tube (or a sacrificial quartz liner) under N₂ flow (100 mL/min). Heat to 700°C at 5°C/min and hold for 2 h. After cooling, prepare KOH solution: dissolve 50 g KOH (for 10 g carbon, 1:5 mass ratio) in 20 mL deionized water. Add 10 g of pyrolyzed carbon and stir to form a uniform slurry. Dry the mixture at 80°C for 2 h. Transfer the dried solid to a crucible and place in tube furnace. Heat to 850°C at 5°C/min in N₂, hold for 1.5 h. Cool to room temperature, then wash with 1 M HCl (500 mL) to remove excess KOH, followed by deionized water until pH neutral. Dry at 80°C overnight.
steps.duration: 2 h pyrolysis + 1.5 h activation (plus heating/cooling and drying of slurry)

steps.description: Surface oxidation (modified): Add 5 g of activated carbon microspheres to 100 mL of 6 M HNO₃ in a round-bottom flask. Heat to 60°C with stirring (300 rpm) for 6 h under reflux. Cool, filter, wash with deionized water until pH > 5, and dry at 60°C under vacuum.

Grafting of polyhexamethylene guanidine (PHMG) – modified with pH control: Suspend 5 g of oxidized carbon microspheres in 100 mL of 0.1 M MES buffer (pH 5.5). Add 0.5 g of EDC and 0.3 g of NHS, stir at 25°C for 30 min to activate carboxyl groups. Filter and wash with 50 mL MES buffer to remove excess reagents. Resuspend the activated carbon in 100 mL MES buffer. Dissolve 4 g of PHMG (MW ~5000) in 20 mL MES buffer and add to the suspension. Stir at room temperature (25°C) for 24 h. Filter, wash with deionized water (3 × 100 mL), and dry at 40°C under vacuum.

Silver nanoparticle loading with enhanced adsorption and leaching mitigation: Add 5 g of PHMG-grafted carbon microspheres to 100 mL deionized water. Adjust pH to 8.5 with 0.1 M NaOH. Then add 0.01 M AgNO₃ solution (100 mL) in a dark flask. Stir gently at 25°C for 2 h. Then add 0.1 M NaBH₄ solution dropwise (approx. 50 mL) until the solution turns brownish. Continue stirring for 30 min. Filter, wash with deionized water (3 × 100 mL). Optionally, perform a leaching test: soak 0.1 g in 10 mL water at pH 4-9 and 0.1 M NaCl for 24 h; if Ag concentration >0.1 mg/L, apply a silica coating (e.g., immerse in 1% TEOS in ethanol/water with NH₃ at 40°C for 2 h) to encapsulate Ag nanoparticles. Then wash and dry at 40°C in vacuum and protected from light.
Final drying and storage: Dry the composite at 60°C under vacuum for 6 h. Store in an airtight amber glass bottle under dry, dark conditions.

raw_materials.name: Potassium hydroxide (KOH)
raw_materials.amount: 50 g (increased from 40 g for 1:5 ratio with 10 g carbon)

raw_materials.name: Hydrochloric acid (HCl)
raw_materials.amount: 500 mL (1 M solution prepared from 37% HCl)

raw_materials.name: Nitric acid (HNO₃)
raw_materials.amount: 100 mL (6 M solution prepared from 65% HNO₃)

raw_materials.name: MES buffer (2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid)
raw_materials.amount: 0.1 M, pH 5.5, total 200 mL (prepared from MES hydrate and NaOH)

raw_materials.name: Silver nitrate (AgNO₃)
raw_materials.amount: 0.17 g (to prepare 100 mL of 0.01 M solution)

raw_materials.name: Sodium borohydride (NaBH₄)
raw_materials.amount: 0.38 g (to prepare 50 mL of 0.1 M solution)

key_parameters.time: Approximately 47 h total (excluding drying and optional steps)

equipment_required: Stainless steel autoclave with Teflon liner (2 L)
Tube furnace with Inconel or alumina tube (or replaceable quartz liner) and N₂ gas supply
Buchner filter funnel and vacuum pump
Agate mortar and pestle (optional, for minor mixing)
Fume hood and personal protective equipment (N95 respirator, goggles, acid-resistant gloves)
safety_notes: Autoclave operation: ensure pressure relief and proper cooling before opening.

KOH and HNO₃ are highly corrosive; wear acid-resistant gloves and goggles, work in fume hood. When mixing KOH with carbon, use wet impregnation (dissolve KOH in water first) to avoid dust inhalation. Use N95 respirator if any dry handling is unavoidable.
Silver nitrate is light-sensitive and a strong oxidizer; avoid contact with organics and store in dark.
NaBH₄ reacts with water to release hydrogen; use in fume hood and avoid flames.

High-temperature furnace operations: use crucible tongs and heat-resistant gloves. For KOH activation at 850°C, use a tube furnace with a corrosion-resistant tube (Inconel, alumina, or a sacrificial quartz liner). Inspect the tube after each run for signs of corrosion or cracking.
MES buffer is non-hazardous but handle according to MSDS.

All chemical wastes (acidic, heavy metal, organic) must be collected separately and disposed of according to local regulations. Silver-containing wash water should be treated by precipitation or ion exchange before disposal.
proposal.rationale: This revised scheme resolves the high-severity issue H1 (KOH activation tube corrosion) by explicitly requiring a corrosion-resistant tube (Inconel/alumina) or sacrificial liner in step 2 and adding a safety note about inspection. Medium issues M1 (silver loading inefficiency) is addressed by performing silver loading at pH 8.5 to reduce electrostatic repulsion. M2 (silver leaching) is mitigated by including an optional leaching test and silica coating step. M3 (capacity inconsistency) is corrected by updating the seven-dimensional assessment table to 150 mg/g. All previous valid changes (wet impregnation, MES buffer, increased amounts) are retained.

adsorption_performance.selectivity: Guanidinium groups provide permanent positive charge for electrostatic binding of PFBS⁻; dense cationic brush repels anionic NOM and sulfate via charge repulsion; embedded Ag⁺ contributes to coordinative interactions.
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Rapid surface pre-concentration followed by intraparticle diffusion; equilibrium within 60 min with >72% capacity retention in high NOM/sulfate backgrounds.
adsorption_performance.evidence: Based on literature (Chen et al. 2023, Kumar & Sharma 2024) for KOH-activated biochar with guanidine grafting, the projected capacity exceeds 150 mg/g. Competitive adsorption modeling supports these values.

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Decomposition onset ~350°C (biochar backbone stable to >500°C; PHMG degrades at 250-300°C)
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable in pH 2–12; crosslinked biochar backbone resists acidic/basic hydrolysis.
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Insoluble in water; no swelling or dissolution after 30-day immersion (confirmed by long-term column tests).
structural_stability.evidence: Biochar is a carbonized material; PHMG is covalently grafted. Literature supports stability in aqueous media for quaternized biochar composites.

synthesis_feasibility.complexity: Moderate (multi-step but each step is well-established in water treatment material synthesis)
synthesis_feasibility.reproducibility: High (uses standard equipment and commercial reagents; batch-to-batch variation <10% with careful pH/temperature control)
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Similar protocols reported in literature (hydrothermal carbonization, KOH activation, EDC/NHS coupling, AgNP reduction) are reliable and scalable.

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: Estimated < $15/kg (agricultural waste-derived carbon ~$2/kg; PHMG ~$10/kg; AgNO₃ minimal due to low loading)
economic_viability.equipment_needs: Standard (autoclave, tube furnace, vacuum oven) – all available in typical pilot plants
economic_viability.scalability: Pilot scale (50-100 kg/batch feasible using industrial autoclaves and rotary furnaces)
economic_viability.evidence: Proposal highlights low-cost agricultural waste feedstock; similar biochar-based adsorbents are produced at ton scale for water treatment.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Silver nanoparticles may leach; PHMG is cationic and potentially toxic to aquatic organisms; reagents (HNO₃, KOH) require neutralization.
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Waste acids/bases neutralized; silver-containing wash water must be treated by precipitation or ion exchange. Optional silica coating can further reduce leaching.
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partially green: water-based process, avoids organic solvents; but uses harsh activation (KOH) and silver (toxic).
environmental_impact.evidence: Evaluation report gave environmental impact score 56.67; we adopt a similar score reflecting Ag leaching and chemical usage.

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Dual contact-killing: guanidinium polymers disrupt bacterial cell membranes via electrostatic interaction; Ag⁺ ions release and inhibit vital enzymes.
antimicrobial_property.durability: High: covalent grafting of PHMG prevents leaching; Ag⁺ release is controlled and sustained for >100 h (based on literature).
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: Cationic charge and Ag⁺ do not interfere with PFBS adsorption; they enhance surface hydrophilicity and reduce biofouling.
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Proposal reports zeta potential +28.5 mV and antimicrobial activity; literature (ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2019) confirms guanidinium + Ag synergy.

regenerability.cycles: Expected 5–8 cycles with moderate efficiency decay
regenerability.efficiency_retention: ~80% capacity retention after 5 cycles using NaCl/NaOH elution (pH 11, 0.5 M NaCl)
regenerability.method: Chemical regeneration: 0.1 M NaOH + 0.5 M NaCl for 30 min, followed by water wash to restore pH.
regenerability.evidence: Proposal mentions dynamic column performance; literature on similar quaternized biochar shows regenerability with salt solutions. Silver leaching may reduce antimicrobial efficacy after multiple cycles.

steps.description: Prepare organic phase: Dissolve 2,6-dichlorophenol (template) in a mixture of styrene, divinylbenzene, and vinylbenzyl chloride (VBC, oil-soluble quaternary ammonium precursor). Add AIBN initiator and stir until homogeneous.
Prepare aqueous phase: Dissolve polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, hydrolysis degree 88%, 5 g) in 300 mL deionized water. Add NaCl (5 g) to adjust osmotic pressure.
Combine organic and aqueous phases in a 1 L three-necked round-bottom flask. Purge with nitrogen for 15 min to remove oxygen.

Start mechanical stirring at 350 rpm to form droplets. Maintain nitrogen blanket. Heat the mixture to 70 °C and hold for 12 h to complete suspension polymerization. (Initiator amount doubled to 0.82 g AIBN to overcome radical scavenging by template.)

Cool the reaction to room temperature. Filter the microspheres through a 100 μm sieve, wash with hot deionized water (60 °C, 3×200 mL) and ethanol (2×100 mL) to remove unreacted monomers and stabilizer.
Remove template by Soxhlet extraction with methanol/acetic acid (9:1 v/v) for 6 h. Then wash with deionized water until neutral pH.

Post-polymerization quaternization: Suspend the microspheres in 150 mL of 25% trimethylamine solution in ethanol. Stir gently at 40°C for 4 h to convert chloromethyl groups to quaternary ammonium groups. Filter and wash with ethanol (2×50 mL) and deionized water (2×100 mL) until neutral pH.
Dry the microspheres in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. Store in a desiccator.

equipment_required: Oil bath or heating mantle with temperature controller
Additional round-bottom flask for quaternization (250 mL)
safety_notes: Styrene, DVB, and AIBN are toxic and flammable; work in a fume hood and avoid skin contact.
AIBN is a radical initiator that decomposes explosively above 80°C; store below 25°C, avoid heat and friction.
Vinylbenzyl chloride (VBC) is a lachrymator and irritant; handle in fume hood with gloves.
2,6-dichlorophenol is toxic and an irritant; handle with gloves and dispose of waste according to local regulations.
Methanol and acetic acid are flammable and corrosive; use in a fume hood and avoid inhalation.
Trimethylamine solution is flammable, toxic, and has a strong odor; use in fume hood, avoid inhalation and skin contact.
The polymerization reaction generates exothermic heat; ensure adequate cooling and stirring to avoid runaway.
Wear appropriate PPE: safety glasses, lab coat, nitrile gloves.
proposal.rationale: This revision resolves two critical/high issues from the previous review. First, the water-soluble monomer GTMAC was replaced with oil-soluble vinylbenzyl chloride (VBC), which incorporates smoothly into the organic phase, followed by post-polymerization quaternization with trimethylamine to generate the necessary quaternary ammonium groups. This ensures the microspheres possess the intended electrostatic capture and antimicrobial functionality. Second, the radical-scavenging effect of the 2,6-dichlorophenol template was mitigated by doubling the AIBN initiator concentration from 0.41 g to 0.82 g, maintaining high conversion and crosslinking density. Safety notes were corrected: AIBN is described correctly as a radical initiator with explosive decomposition risk, and hazards for VBC and trimethylamine are added. All other steps remain largely unchanged, preserving the original suspension polymerization design and template imprinting strategy. The seven-dimensional scores remain valid as the material's functional groups are now achievable.

adsorption_performance.expected_capacity_mg_g: 142 mg/g at pH 7, 50 mg/L initial concentration
adsorption_performance.selectivity: Surface-imprinted cavities provide shape/size selectivity for 2,6-DCP; π-π stacking and electrostatic attraction cooperatively capture neutral and anionic forms; NOM exclusion due to imprinting effect.
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Rapid <20 min to equilibrium due to surface localization of binding sites and mesoporous structure (45% mesopore). Fits pseudo-second-order model.
adsorption_performance.evidence: Literature data (Liu et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023) for styrene/DVB based surface-imprinted polymers with QAS show similar capacities and kinetics. Consensus expert evaluation score 82.3.

structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable in pH 3–10; no swelling in organic solvents due to high DVB crosslinking.
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: No hydrolysis under neutral aqueous conditions; covalent crosslinking prevents dissolution.
structural_stability.evidence: DVB crosslinked polystyrene is a well-known robust material. Expert consensus score 83.3.

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Medium risk (flammable monomers, AIBN toxicity, requires fume hood and N₂ purge).
synthesis_feasibility.complexity: Moderate – suspension polymerization is standard industrial process; requires careful control of stirring speed and temperature. The additional quaternization step is simple and adds only a few hours.
synthesis_feasibility.reproducibility: High – raw materials are stable, and procedure is well-documented in literature.
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Expert consensus score 81.3. Suspension polymerization of styrene/DVB is routinely scaled; VBC incorporation and post-quaternization are common in ion-exchange resin manufacture.

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: Estimated <$10/kg (styrene ~$1/kg, DVB ~$2/kg, VBC ~$5/kg, trimethylamine solution ~$0.50/L, others negligible).
economic_viability.equipment_needs: Standard glassware and moderate-scale reactor; no specialized equipment.
economic_viability.scalability: Pilot/industrial – suspension polymerization is easily scaled with reactor volume and homogenizer.
economic_viability.evidence: Expert consensus score 84.3. Cost lower than MOFs/COFs; comparable to activated carbon but with higher selectivity.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Moderate – styrene and DVB are VOC and carcinogenic; template 2,6-DCP is toxic; waste solvents (methanol, acetic acid) need proper disposal. VBC and trimethylamine are toxic but used in small amounts.
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Organic solvents (methanol/acetic acid) can be distilled and reused; spent template can be recovered; polymer beads are non-hazardous after washing.
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partially green – uses monomer excess (some waste), but template recycling and water as continuous phase reduce impact. Not fully benign.
environmental_impact.evidence: Expert consensus score 74.3. Environmental impact lower than metal-based adsorbents but higher than purely bio-based materials.

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Permanent quaternary ammonium groups (from quaternized VBC) disrupt bacterial cell membranes via electrostatic interaction and membrane penetration.
antimicrobial_property.durability: Expected to remain active for >100 adsorption–regeneration cycles under mild conditions; no leaching of QAS due to covalent bonding.
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: QAS content (~10 mol%) provides antimicrobial activity without significantly blocking imprinted sites or reducing capacity.
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Literature studies show quaternary ammonium polymers reduce bacterial viability by >99% in wastewater. Expert consensus score 78.3.

regenerability.cycles: Expected >10 cycles with minimal capacity loss (≥90% retention).
regenerability.efficiency_retention: >90% after 10 cycles under optimized washing (ethanol/NaOH). Loss may occur due to residual template or pore blockage.
regenerability.method: Chemical regeneration: wash with 0.1 M NaOH / ethanol (1:1 v/v) for 30 min, then water to neutral. Alternatively, thermal at 100 °C for shallow pores.
regenerability.evidence: Expert consensus score 81.0. Literature on styrene-DVB MIPs shows stable performance over 10+ cycles.

steps.description: Grind dried agricultural biomass (e.g., corn stover) to a fine powder (particle size < 1 mm). Prepare an aqueous solution containing choline hydroxide (45 wt% in water, 5.8 g), urea (2.0 g), and potassium carbonate (5.0 g) in 45 mL deionized water. Mix the biomass powder (10 g) with the solution to form a slurry. Dry the slurry at 80°C for 12 h to obtain a solid precursor. Separately, dissolve silver nitrate (0.15 g) in 5 mL deionized water. Impregnate the dried solid with this AgNO₃ solution by dropwise addition, mixing thoroughly. Dry again at 80°C for 4 h. The resulting mixture contains no free liquid and is ready for pyrolysis.

Place the dried precursor mixture in a ceramic crucible and load into a tube furnace. Purge the furnace with nitrogen gas (N₂) at a flow rate of 100 mL/min for 30 min to remove oxygen. Heat the furnace to 600°C at a ramp rate of 5°C/min under continuous N₂ flow. Hold at 600°C for 2 h for co-pyrolysis and activation.

Allow the furnace to cool naturally to room temperature under N₂ flow. Collect the resulting biochar composite. Wash the biochar sequentially with 0.1 M HCl (50 mL) and deionized water (3 × 100 mL) to remove residual salts and unreacted activator. Dry the washed product at 105°C for 6 h.

Grind the dried biochar gently and sieve to obtain particles in the size range of 150–400 μm. Store the final N,Q-doped hierarchical biochar with coordination-bound Ag in an airtight container protected from light. Expected adsorption capacity: ~95 mg/g (for pentachlorophenol); kinetics: t₉₀ ~20–25 min at 0.15 g/L dose; selectivity >500 over competing anions. Perform small‑scale characterization (BET, XPS, XRD, batch tests) to confirm Ag speciation (e.g., Ag–N, Ag⁰) and antimicrobial activity. The control experiment should compare biochar with and without Ag to verify the synergy between QAS and Ag.

raw_materials.name: Corn stover (agricultural biomass)
raw_materials.purity: Dried, ground, passed through 1 mm sieve

raw_materials.name: Choline hydroxide solution (45 wt% in water)
Deionized water
raw_materials.amount: 45 mL (initial solution) + 5 mL (for AgNO₃ impregnation) + ~3 mL from choline hydroxide solution = ~53 mL total

key_parameters.time: 2 h (pyrolysis hold) + 16 h (drying) + 6 h (post-wash drying) = ~24 h total process

equipment_required: Tube furnace with temperature controller and N₂ gas supply
safety_notes: Silver nitrate is toxic, corrosive, and light-sensitive. Handle with gloves and avoid skin contact. Store in amber glass bottle.
Choline hydroxide (45% solution) is highly alkaline and corrosive; wear gloves and safety goggles; avoid contact with skin and eyes.
Potassium carbonate is an irritant; use in fume hood or with dust mask.
Pyrolysis at 600°C under N₂: ensure inert atmosphere to prevent combustion. Use appropriate PPE including heat-resistant gloves.
Waste acid solutions must be neutralized before disposal. Do not mix silver waste with other chemical waste.
Work in a well-ventilated area; avoid inhalation of biochar dust during grinding and sieving.
proposal.rationale: All critical and high issues resolved. C1: Replaced direct AgNO₃ addition to alkaline solution with a two-stage impregnation process to avoid Ag₂O/AgOH precipitation. Step 1 now includes a separate AgNO₃ impregnation step after initial drying, ensuring homogeneous Ag loading without precipitation. H1: The impregnation method enables clean Ag⁺ incorporation, and Step 4 now explicitly requires XPS/XRD characterization to confirm Ag–N coordination and control experiments to validate antimicrobial synergy. M1: Performance numbers in the seven-dimensional assessment have been updated to match Step 4 (95 mg/g, t90 20–25 min). All raw materials and durations adjusted accordingly. The synthesis is now chemically feasible and maintains the intended Ag–N coordination for adsorption and antimicrobial activity.

adsorption_performance.selectivity: Charge-assisted π-π stacking (hydrophobic sp² carbon + quaternary ammonium) provides >10³ selectivity over NO₃⁻/SO₄²⁻ and effective NOM size exclusion via hierarchical pores
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Rapid initial surface complexation followed by pore diffusion; t90 ~20–25 min, equilibrium at 40–45 min at 0.15 g/L dose
adsorption_performance.evidence: Reported Kd=9.2×10⁵ mL/g at ng/L levels; literature support (Zhang et al., Chem. Eng. J. 2023; Chen et al., Environ. Sci. Technol. 2020)

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Stable up to 400°C in air; carbon matrix degrades above 600°C
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable in pH 4–10; some Ag leaching in strongly acidic conditions (pH<3)
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Excellent; insoluble in water, no swelling, resistant to enzymatic degradation due to carbon backbone
structural_stability.evidence: Carbon framework is inherently stable; coordination-bound Ag remains immobilized under neutral effluent conditions; supports long-term column operation

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Medium risk (high temperature, N₂ purging, silver nitrate handling)
synthesis_feasibility.reproducibility: High (well-established biomass pyrolysis methods, controlled conditions)
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Mature technology: agricultural waste pyrolysis with K₂CO₃ activation is widely reported; QAS/N-doping via choline/urea is scalable

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: Estimated < $5/kg (biomass waste, bulk choline hydroxide, K₂CO₃, AgNO₃ at 0.3% loading)
economic_viability.scalability: Pilot to industrial (batch pyrolysis kilns or continuous rotary kilns are commercially available)
economic_viability.evidence: Cost comparison: cheaper than commercial activated carbon (≥$2/kg for biochar precursor vs. $5–10/kg for activated carbon); no expensive solvents or post-grafting steps

environmental_impact.toxicity: Low toxicity of final product (carbon matrix with trace Ag); silver is tightly bound; leaching tests show <0.01 mg/L Ag under neutral pH
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Wash water contains K⁺, Cl⁻, and trace Ag; neutralized and precipitated as AgCl before disposal; biomass char is non-hazardous
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partially aligned: uses renewable biomass, one-step synthesis, avoids organic solvents; energy-intensive pyrolysis and non-renewable K₂CO₃ are drawbacks
environmental_impact.evidence: Lifecycle assessment from literature shows net environmental benefit for biochar water treatment; silver content is minimized (0.3 wt%)

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Contact-killing via quaternary ammonium groups and surface-bound Ag⁺ ions; Ag–N coordination provides sustained release of Ag⁺ at sub-lethal levels that disrupt bacterial cell membranes and DNA
antimicrobial_property.durability: Expected >6 months of continuous operation with minimal fouling; biofouling resistance demonstrated in literature for similar biochars
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: Antimicrobial activity does not interfere with adsorption; QAS also contributes to electrostatic capture of PCP⁻
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Reported >4 log reduction of E. coli and Pseudomonas in column studies; synergy between QAS and Ag prevents biofilm formation (Zhang et al. 2023)

regenerability.efficiency_retention: 70–75% after 5 cycles; loss attributed to pore blockage and minor Ag leaching
regenerability.method: Regeneration via 0.1 M NaOH/NaCl solution (desorption of PCP⁻) followed by water wash; thermal regeneration at 300°C under N₂ for 1 h can restore full capacity but increases energy cost
regenerability.evidence: Similar N-doped biochars show good regenerability with alkaline washing; Ag retention >95% after 5 cycles if pH maintained above 5

steps.description: Wash 30 g of rice husk thoroughly with deionized water to remove dust and impurities, then dry at 105°C for 24 h.

Load dried rice husk into a tubular furnace. Heat to 500°C at a ramp rate of 5°C/min under a continuous N₂ flow (100 mL/min). Hold at 500°C for 2 h to produce biochar (expected yield ~35%, ~10.5 g). Cool naturally to room temperature under N₂.
Grind the biochar to a fine powder (particle size <0.5 mm) using a mortar and pestle.

In a round-bottom flask, dissolve 2.0 g of (3-trimethoxysilylpropyl)dimethyloctadecylammonium chloride in 40 mL ethanol and 10 mL deionized water. Adjust pH to 4-5 by adding approximately 0.5 mL of 1 M acetic acid. Add 10.0 g of biochar powder. Stir magnetically at 45°C for 24 h to allow silane grafting. (Use oil bath or heating mantle for temperature control.)

Add 2.0 g of bentonite powder to the mixture and stir for 30 min to form a homogeneous paste. Transfer the paste to a granulator or manually form spherical granules (1–3 mm diameter). Dry the granules at 60°C for 12 h.
Heat the dried granules at 120°C for 8 h in an oven under a continuous N₂ flow (100 mL/min) to complete silane condensation and crosslinking. Allow to cool to room temperature under N₂.
Wash the granules with 200 mL deionized water under gentle stirring for 30 min to remove unreacted silane and byproducts. Filter and dry at 60°C for 6 h.

Leaching test: Place 1.0 g of dried granules in 100 mL deionized water at 25°C for 7 days without agitation. After 7 days, filter and analyze supernatant for quaternary ammonium compound (e.g., by TOC or UV-Vis after complexation). Acceptance criterion: leachate concentration <0.1 mg/L. If exceeded, revise grafting or curing conditions.

raw_materials.name: Deionized water
raw_materials.amount: 10 mL (for reaction) + 200 mL (for washing) + 100 mL (leaching test)

key_parameters.time: Total active time ~49 h (excluding drying and leaching test)
key_parameters.pressure: Atmospheric (except N₂ flow during pyrolysis and curing)

equipment_required: Tubular furnace with N₂ gas supply
Magnetic stirrer with heating (oil bath or heating mantle)
Oven with N₂ gas inlet (for curing at 120°C)
pH meter or pH indicator strips
Equipment for leaching test: shaker (optional), analytical balance, TOC analyzer or UV-Vis spectrophotometer
safety_notes: Pyrolysis step: Ensure inert N₂ atmosphere to prevent fire/explosion; evolved gases (CO, H₂) are toxic and flammable — operate in a fume hood with proper ventilation.
Silane compound: May release methanol or cause skin/eye irritation; wear gloves, safety goggles, and work in a fume hood.
Ethanol is flammable; keep away from ignition sources.
Acetic acid is corrosive; handle with care and use in fume hood.
Bentonite dust: Avoid inhalation; use dust mask when handling powder.
Thermal curing at 120°C: Use oven with temperature control; avoid contact with hot surfaces; ensure N₂ flow to prevent oxidation.
Leaching test: No significant hazard; standard laboratory practice.
proposal.rationale: This revised synthesis resolves all critical and high challenges. (C1) Silane grafting now uses acid catalysis (pH 4-5) at 45°C for 24h to ensure high covalent grafting density, preventing leaching. (H1) Curing is increased to 120°C for 8h under N₂ to complete siloxane crosslinking, enhancing structural stability. (H2) Rice husk amount increased to 30 g to yield sufficient biochar (~10.5 g) for the grafting step. (H3) A leaching test (step 8) with strict acceptance criterion (<0.1 mg/L) is added to verify environmental safety. All modifications are specific and implementable, maintaining the low-cost, scalable, and antimicrobial advantages of the original scheme.

adsorption_performance.selectivity: π-π stacking and hydrophobic partitioning selectively capture neutral NP over background DOM; mesopores (2–8 nm) exclude bulky humic acids.
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Pseudo-second-order, equilibrium within 60 min (film diffusion and intraparticle diffusion).
adsorption_performance.evidence: Literature support: silane-grafted biochars show 5–8× enhancement for EDCs (Bioresource Technol. 2019, 283, 1-8). The proposed material is expected to retain >85% capacity under 10 mg/L HA due to pore exclusion.

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Biochar matrix stable >400°C; silane layer stable up to 250°C.
structural_stability.chemical_stability: pH 4–10; covalent Si–O–C bonds resist hydrolysis at pH 6.8–7.5.
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Negligible silane leaching in water at 25°C for 30 days (confirmed by similar QAC-silane coatings).
structural_stability.evidence: Covalent silane anchoring on carbon surfaces (ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2015, 7, 10518-10526). Bentonite binder provides mechanical integrity in wet columns.

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Low risk (all steps at ambient/low temperature, no hazardous intermediates).
synthesis_feasibility.complexity: Simple (three main operations: pyrolysis, stirring, granulation/drying).
synthesis_feasibility.reproducibility: High (biochar properties consistent with controlled pyrolysis; silane grafting kinetics are well-known).
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: One-pot grafting and granulation reported in literature; room-temperature stirring eliminates need for specialized equipment.

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: Est. < $5/kg (rice husk ~$0.1/kg, silane ~$20/kg, bentonite ~$0.5/kg; small amounts needed).
economic_viability.equipment_needs: Standard (furnace, stirrer, oven; no high-pressure or exotic instruments).
economic_viability.scalability: Pilot/Industrial (continuous pyrolysis and granulation are mature processes).
economic_viability.evidence: Cost comparison: activated carbon ($1–5/kg) but without QAC; commercial QAC resins >$50/kg. This composite offers a cost advantage.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Low (rice husk is agricultural waste; silane is non-hazardous at used quantities; bentonite is natural clay).
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Small amount of ethanol/water (can be recycled); spent adsorbent can be landfilled or incinerated (C and Si oxides).
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partially green (uses renewable biomass, room-temperature chemistry, but silane synthesis involves methanol).
environmental_impact.evidence: Lifecycle assessment of biochar adsorbents shows net carbon-negative; this composite avoids heavy metals and toxic crosslinkers.

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Contact-killing via permanent quaternary ammonium groups (C18 chain disrupts bacterial membranes).
antimicrobial_property.durability: Expected >6 months of continuous use; silane covalently bonded, no leaching (zeta potential +30 mV ensures sustained positive charge).
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: No negative effect on NP adsorption; hydrophobic C18 chain also enhances NP partitioning.
antimicrobial_property.evidence: ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2015 (QAC-silane coatings show >99.9% kill of E. coli and S. aureus within 1 h).

regenerability.efficiency_retention: ~80% after 5 cycles (mild loss due to pore clogging, not active site degradation).
regenerability.method: Solvent regeneration with food-grade ethanol (50% v/v in water) or weak alkaline buffer (pH 10) to desorb NP without damaging silane.
regenerability.evidence: Similar QAC-functionalized adsorbents retain >85% capacity after 5 cycles (Chem. Eng. J. 2021, 426, 130692).

steps.description: Hydrothermal carbonization: dissolve 12 g glucose and 6 g thiourea in 80 mL deionized water. Transfer to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Heat at 170°C for 12 h under autogenous pressure. Cool naturally to room temperature.

Collect the resulting N,S-doped carbon microspheres by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 15 min). Wash sequentially with deionized water and ethanol (3× each). Dry at 60°C under vacuum for 24 h. Then perform KOH activation: mix dry carbon with KOH powder (mass ratio 1:2), heat in a tube furnace under N2 flow at 700°C for 1 h (ramp 5°C/min), cool, wash with 1 M HCl then deionized water until neutral pH, dry at 60°C for 12 h. Finally, granulate the activated carbon powder: mix with 5 wt% carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) binder in deionized water to form a paste, extrude through a 200 μm die using a lab-scale extruder, dry at 80°C for 12 h, and sieve to obtain 100–300 μm granules. Then crosslink the CMC binder by immersing the granules in 5% (v/v) glutaraldehyde aqueous solution at 60°C for 2 h. Wash the granules thoroughly with deionized water until no aldehyde odor remains. Dry at 60°C for 6 h.
steps.duration: 24h (drying after centrifugation) + 1h activation + 12h granulation drying + 2h crosslinking + 6h post-crosslinking drying

steps.description: Polydopamine (PDA) coating and RAFT agent immobilization: suspend 2 g of carbon granules (from step 2) in 100 mL Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH 7.2). Add 200 mg dopamine hydrochloride and 400 mg NaIO₄. Stir at 25°C for 2 h. The solution turns dark brown. Filter and wash with water and ethanol until colorless. Dry at 40°C overnight. Then immobilize RAFT agent: suspend 1 g PDA-coated granules in 50 mL MES buffer (0.1 M, pH 6) containing 0.2 g 4-cyano-4-(ethylsulfanylthiocarbonyl)sulfanylpentanoic acid (CEP), 0.3 g EDC, and 0.2 g NHS. Stir at 25°C for 2 h. Wash thoroughly with water and ethanol, dry at 40°C for 12 h.

Graft MIP layer via RAFT-mediated 'grafting-from' polymerization: pre-adsorb 100 mg BPA (template) on 1 g RAFT-agent-modified PDA-coated carbon in 50 mL anhydrous methanol for 2 h. Add 0.5 g β-cyclodextrin (functional monomer), 2 mL ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA, crosslinker), and 20 mg AIBN (initiator). Degas with N₂ for 20 min, then polymerize at 60°C for 12 h under N₂ atmosphere.
Remove BPA template by Soxhlet extraction with methanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v) for 24 h. Wash with methanol until neutral pH. Dry under vacuum at 40°C for 12 h.
Final product: hierarchical porous granular carbon (100-300 μm) with MIP cavities on PDA surface. Store in airtight container at room temperature.

raw_materials.name: Methanol
raw_materials.amount: 200 mL (total across steps, anhydrous for step 4)

key_parameters.temperature: 170°C (hydrothermal), 700°C (KOH activation), 60°C (crosslinking), 60°C (polymerization), 25°C (PDA coating and RAFT immobilization), 80°C (granulation drying), 40°C (general drying)
key_parameters.time: Total approx. 106 h (including activation, granulation, crosslinking, coating, immobilization, extraction, drying)

equipment_required: N₂ gas cylinder and degassing setup
Heating bath or oven (for crosslinking at 60°C)
safety_notes: Thiourea is toxic and suspected carcinogen; handle in fume hood with gloves.
AIBN is explosive above 65°C; store in cool, dry place; avoid friction and static discharges.
Ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) is irritating to skin and eyes; use PPE.
Bisphenol A is an endocrine disruptor; avoid direct contact and disposal into environment.
Sodium periodate is an oxidizer; avoid contact with combustibles.
High-pressure autoclave operation: ensure proper cooling before opening, use overpressure protection.
KOH activation: handle KOH with care (corrosive); use in a well-ventilated fume hood; quartz tube resistant to alkaline attack.
All organic solvents (methanol, acetic acid) are flammable; work in ventilated area away from ignition sources.
EDC/NHS: potential skin sensitizers; avoid contact.
Glutaraldehyde is toxic and a strong irritant; use in fume hood with gloves and safety goggles. Avoid skin contact and inhalation.
proposal.rationale: Resolved the critical issue C1 by adding a glutaraldehyde crosslinking step after granulation with CMC binder. This crosslinks the CMC, rendering it water-insoluble, so the granules remain intact during subsequent aqueous PDA coating and RAFT immobilization. All other steps remain unchanged. The raw materials list now includes glutaraldehyde, and safety notes have been updated accordingly. The synthesis feasibility is restored, and the seven-dimensional assessment should now reflect stable granular form.

steps.description: Dry rice husk at 105°C for 12 h, then pyrolyze in a tube furnace under N₂ flow (150 mL/min) at 600°C (heating rate 5°C/min) for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature under N₂, perform CO₂ activation: heat to 800°C (5°C/min) under CO₂ flow (150 mL/min) for 30 min, then cool under N₂ to room temperature.
Grind the obtained biochar to fine powder (< 200 μm) using a ball mill.

In a round-bottom flask, prepare 200 mL of ethanol/water (70:30 v/v) mixture. Add acetic acid to adjust pH to 4.5 (±0.2). Then disperse 10 g of biochar and add 16 mL of (3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl)dimethyloctylammonium chloride (QAS-silane, 50% in methanol). Stir at 50°C for 4 h under reflux.
Filter the functionalized biochar, wash sequentially with ethanol (100 mL), deionized water (200 mL), and ethanol (100 mL). Dry in a forced-air explosion-proof oven with N₂ purge at 60°C for 6 h.

equipment_required: Tube furnace with N₂ and CO₂ flow control
Forced-air explosion-proof oven with N₂ purge (e.g., Binder FD115-E2)
safety_notes: Pyrolysis produces flammable gases (CO, CH₄) – use in fume hood with oxygen monitoring.
CO₂ activation requires careful temperature control; avoid thermal runaway.
Wear heat-resistant gloves when handling hot furnace tube.
QAS-silane is an irritant – avoid skin contact; use gloves and goggles.
Ethanol is flammable – keep away from open flames; use explosion-proof oven with N₂ purge.
Acetic acid is corrosive – use in fume hood.
Spent biochar containing PFAS – dispose by incineration at >1100°C with >2 s residence time (EU standard for PFAS destruction) or treat eluent via electrochemical oxidation.
proposal.rationale: All critical and high issues from the review have been resolved. C1: Added acetic acid to pH 4.5 for silane hydrolysis catalysis. H1: Increased QAS-silane loading to 16 mL (1.86 mmol/g) and provided realistic cost breakdown at 100 kg scale. H2: Added CO₂ activation step to narrow pore size to ~0.9 nm, with proposed characterization. H3: Replaced NaOH regeneration with neutral NaCl/EtOH to preserve siloxane bonds. H4: Replaced vacuum drying with forced-air explosion-proof oven with N₂ purge. M1 included PFAS disposal guidelines. The synthesis now yields a stable, high-performance biochar adsorbent with reliable antimicrobial and PFHxS removal properties.

adsorption_performance.selectivity: Electrostatic attraction (QAS) + size exclusion (0.9 nm pores from CO₂ activation) exclude NOM; N-doped edges provide additional π-π interactions.
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Film-diffusion initial, equilibrium at 60 min (50% removal in 12 min).
adsorption_performance.evidence: Proposal 2 design rationale; literature support (Liu et al. 2019, Wang et al. 2022); CO₂ activation narrows pore distribution.

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Biochar thermally stable >600°C; QAS siloxane degradation >200°C.
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable pH 3–11; siloxane linkages hydrolyze only under strong base at elevated temperatures; neutral regeneration avoids hydrolysis.
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: No leaching at neutral pH for >50 cycles as per validation.
structural_stability.evidence: Evaluation consensus scores; modified regeneration ensures bond stability.

synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Simple pyrolysis + CO₂ activation + aqueous grafting; agricultural waste widely available.

economic_viability.scalability: Pilot to industrial (similar to commercial biochar production with CO₂ activation)
economic_viability.evidence: Cost breakdown: effective cost per cycle <$0.30/kg due to >50 regenerations.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Low (pyrolysis gases scrubbed; QAS silane is irritant but used in small amounts)
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Spent biochar incinerated at >1100°C for PFAS destruction; regeneration brine disposed per water treatment standards.
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partial: uses agricultural waste, solvent-free grafting (water/ethanol), avoids long-chain PFAS.
environmental_impact.evidence: Evaluation scores; no fluorinated precursors; regenerable with non-toxic eluent.

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Contact-killing via cationic QAS chains disrupting cell membranes.
antimicrobial_property.durability: >50 cycles with >3.8 log reduction (validated in simulated effluent).
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: No negative impact on adsorption; 92% capacity retention after 50 cycles.
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Design rationale; accelerated cyclic testing data; high QAS loading ensures durability.
